content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_1">, with <foreign xml:lang="lat">cognovissem</foreign> (1.5), prot. cont. to fact, with <foreign xml:lang="lat">censerem</foreign> (1.7) as its apod. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_1">, correl. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sic_1"> (1.3). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_litteris_1">, i.e. despatches from the seat of war. <milestone n="242.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostium_1">, i.e. Antony's forces. <milestone n="242.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_1">, namely </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="D_esse_1"> (1.4). <milestone n="242.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Brutum_1">: D. Brutus, one of Caesar's murderers, had been assigned by him to the government of Cisalpine Gaul, and took possession of the province after Caesar's death. In the summer, Antony procured the passage of a law transferring this province to himself. Brutus, supported by the Senate, refused to give it up, and upon this issue hostilities broke out. Brutus was at this time besieged in Mutina (Modena), and the consuls, Hirtius and Pansa, had moved to raise the siege. <milestone n="242.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_saga_1">, etc., as we should say figuratively "to arms." The <foreign xml:lang="lat">sagum</foreign> (Fig. 51) was a simple woollen cloak, fastened over one shoulder with a clasp or buckle (<foreign xml:lang="lat">fibula</foreign>), while the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> toga</foreign> had no fastening but was wound in elaborate folds about the body. It was put on instead of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign> (the garment of peace: see note on <ref n="pl125.17">p. 125, l. 17</ref>) in the city when there was war near home, as a sign that the citizens were called to arms. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="issemus_1">, subj. of subord. clause in indir. disc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="redeundum_1">, etc.: to return to the ordinary garb of peace, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign>, would, under the circumstances, be a sign of rejoicing. <milestone n="242.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ea_res_1">, i.e. the liberation of D. Brutus from siege. <milestone n="242.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pugnae_1">: the victory of Hirtius and Pansa at Bononia (see Introd., p.241 of text). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ista_sententia_2"> , <gloss>that proposition</gloss> (one proposed by the Senator P. Servilius, and opposed by Cicero in this oration). <milestone n="242.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_ut_2">, etc., <gloss>let us do so with the intention to retain it</gloss>. <milestone n="242.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_2">, referring to <foreign xml:lang="lat">discedere</foreign> (1.18). The point is that it would not be pleasing to the gods for the citizens to assume the garb of rejoicing merely for a day, and then, since their main prayer had not been granted, to return <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad saga</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="redierimus_3">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad vestitum</foreign>. <milestone n="242.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_prodatur_3">, i.e. if they change their attire for this one day, it will appear that it was not on account of Brutus that the change was made, for he is not yet safe. <milestone n="243.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tollite_hanc_3"> , <gloss>set aside this motive</gloss>: a kind of <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, b (310, b); B. 305, 2; G. 593, 4; H. 560, 4, N. (487, 3); H.-B. 497, 2. <milestone n="243.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conservate_3">, etc., <gloss>maintain your dignity</gloss> (by sustaining Brutus). <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legati_4">: this was in January. At the head of the embassy was the distinguished jurist, Ser. Sulpicius Galba, who died on the journey. <milestone n="243.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hosti_4">, i.e. Antony. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="denuntiarent_4"> , <gloss>order</gloss> (with threats). <milestone n="243.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Hirtius_4">, the consul (see Introd., p.241 of text). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imbecillitatem_4"> , <gloss>infirm condition</gloss>. Cicero had said of him before: "How feeble and worn he was! But the infirmity of his body did not check the vigor of his soul." <milestone n="243.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Caesar_4">, i.e. Octavianus. <milestone n="243.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liberasset_4">: Octavianus had taken an active part in the autumn in thwarting Antony's plans. <milestone n="243.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dolorem_domesticum_4"> , <gloss>some private grief</gloss> i.e. for the death of Julius Caesar, his adoptive father. It should be remembered that D. Brutus was one of the assassins of Caesar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_egit_5"> , <gloss>what abject had Pansa?</gloss> He had set out for Mutina some weeks after his colleague Hirtius. <milestone n="243.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faciendis_5"> , <gloss>procuring</gloss> (i.e. as presiding officer of the Senate). <milestone n="243.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessitati_victus_5">, implying that the war brought distress in the provision market. <milestone n="243.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_5">, i.e. the liberation of Brutus from siege. <milestone n="243.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="et_5"> connects </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_5"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evento_5">. <milestone n="243.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeripuisse_5"> , <gloss>seized prematurely</gloss>, if the news proved true; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contempsisse_5"> , <gloss>scorned</gloss>, if it proved false. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="significatio_vestra_6"> , <gloss>the indication you have giver'</gloss> (i.e. by signs of approval). <milestone n="243.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propraetore_6">: Octavianus, upon whom the Senate had specially conferred this rank early in January. He was left in sole command after the death of Hirtius and Pansa. -51... ante, <gloss>as soon as</gloss>. <milestone n="243.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pertineant_6">: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 508, 3; H. 649, (528, I); H.-B. 535, I, a. <milestone n="243.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercituumque_6">: this term is added because the legions contained only Romans, while the consular armies had also auxiliaries. <milestone n="243.32" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duobus_6">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">proeliis</foreign>. The battle was begun by Pansa, who was routed and mortally wounded, although the fatal character of his wound was not yet known at Rome; then the fortune of the day was retrieved by reinforcements led by Hirtius. Octavianus took no part in this engagement, but repulsed an attack upon the camp. <milestone n="244.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostium_6">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">civium</foreign>: Cicero's great point in the Philippics is to make out that Antony — like Catiline — is no citizen, but a public enemy. In the argument that follows he shows that the proposition of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">supplicatio</foreign> (see note, Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.15">15</ref>, <ref n="pl133.19">p. 133, l. 19</ref>), which had never been decreed except for a victory over foreign enemies, endorses this view by treating Antony as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">hostis</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostium_6_2">, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summa_pietas_6"> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nefarium_scelus_6"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civium_6">: observe the chiastic order. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostem_7">: the proposition seems to have studiously omitted calling Antony's troops <gloss> enemies</gloss>: this Cicero objects to. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vero_7">, <gloss> forsooth</gloss>, marks the irony. <milestone n="244.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civium_7">: if not <foreign xml:lang="lat">hostes</foreign>, they were, of course, <foreign xml:lang="lat">cives</foreign>, whom it would be impious to kill. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="improbis_7"> (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">civibus</foreign>), <gloss>criminals</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inquit_7">: the mover of the proposition which Cicero is combating is supposed to retort that, though citizens, these are criminals, and that Cicero's sarcasm therefore misses fire. <milestone n="244.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="clarissimus_vir_7">: P. Servilius Vatia, the proposer of the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> supplicatio</foreign>, Caesar's colleague in his second consulship, <date when="-0048">B.C. 48</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_7">, etc.: i.e. these words are appropriate not to soldiers in arms against the state but to civil offenders. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bellum_8">, etc.: this is Cicero's statement of the real facts as opposed to his ironical suggestion in the preceding sentence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infert_8">, used of offensive war. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quattuor_consulibus_8">, i.e. besides the consuls, the two consuls elect, Plancus and D. Brutus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unus_8">, i.e. Antony. <milestone n="244.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gent_8">, <gloss>is actually carrying on</gloss>. <milestone n="244.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suis_cladibus_8"> , <gloss>the evils he himself threatens</gloss>. <milestone n="244.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Dolabellae_facinus_8">: Dolabella, Antony's colleague in the consulship, when on his way to the province of Syria, in February, 43, assaulted Smyrna by treachery, captured the <foreign xml:lang="lat">propraetor</foreign> of Asia, C. Trebonius (one of the conspirators against Caesar), and put him to death with indignities and torture. <milestone n="244.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_templo_8">, i.e. that of Jupiter Capitolinus, where the Senate was now met (cf. Cat. 1, sect. 1 and note). <milestone n="244.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Parmensium_8">: Parma had been captured by L. Antonius, and treated in the manner here described. <milestone n="245.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Antonius_8">, the youngest brother of Mark Antony (cos. <date when="-0041">B.C. 41</date> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oblita_9">, from <foreign xml:lang="lat">oblino</foreign>. <milestone n="245.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="crudelitatem_9">: the cruelty of the Carthaginians was proverbial—at least among their enemies the Romans. <milestone n="245.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="capta_9">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">surrepta</foreign>: observe the antithesis. Violence which was excusable in the case of a city taken by storm was, Cicero implies, disgraceful in the case of one taken by treachery. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_urbis_10">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">eum esse</foreign>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbis_10"> limits </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_10"> in the same sense in which <foreign xml:lang="lat">coloniarum</foreign> limits <foreign xml:lang="lat">hostis</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="explendas_10"> , <gloss>replenishing.</gloss> <milestone n="245.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrocini_10"> , <gloss>gang of robbers</gloss>. <milestone n="245.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Saxa_10">, L. Decidius: a Celtiberian by birth, originally a land surveyor, a creature of Caesar's and now of Antony's. The reference here is to a law of Antony, passed in the June preceding, for the establishment of colonies of veterans. In </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="peritus_decempeda_10"> Cicero alludes to Saxa's humble origin and also implies that, in laying out confiscated territory, he habitually appropriated more than the forfeited area. <milestone n="245.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rumoribus_10">, i.e. of the success of Antony's arms. <milestone n="245.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="larem_10">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lar familiaris</foreign> was the protector of the family, and especially of the hearth. <milestone n="245.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_quibus_10"> , <gloss>from whom</gloss> (not <gloss>by whom</gloss>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decreverit_11"> , <gloss>has moved</gloss>. <milestone n="245.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnino_numerum_11"> , <gloss>the total number</gloss>. <milestone n="246.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cui_11">, interrogative. <milestone n="246.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_non_11">, etc., <gloss>without his being called</gloss>. <pb n="488"/> <milestone n="5" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_quis_12">, etc., equiv. to <gloss>or, when, if any one had</gloss>, etc., <gloss>the Senate would have called hint <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator</foreign>, shall we take away</gloss>, etc.? The Latin expresses the thought by two coordinate interrogative sentences: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appellaret_senatus_12"> (with its <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis si quis occidisset</foreign>) and <foreign xml:lang="lat">adimemus</foreign> (with its modifiers). In English it is more natural to make the first of these sentences subordinate. Cf. a similar construction in Manil. Law, sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 58">58</ref>. <milestone n="246.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_increbuit_12">: in the later days of the republic the title of <foreign xml:lang="lat"> imperator</foreign> and the honor of a triumph were granted on much slighter grounds than in earlier times. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appellaret_12"> , <gloss>would have styled</gloss> (imperf. because of repeated action). <milestone n="246.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="isti_domestici_12">, i.e. the partisans of Antony remaining in Rome. <milestone n="246.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ovantem_12">: evidently some informal demonstration of joy on the part of the citizens is referred to, in which Cicero, as a well-known champion of the Senate, was escorted to the Capitol to give thanks to the gods. Strictly the <foreign xml:lang="lat">ovatio</foreign> was an inferior triumph, sometimes granted by the Senate in cases when the proportions or circumstances of the victory, or the rank of the commander, did not warrant the supreme honor of a triumph (see note on <ref n="pl70.17">p. 70, l. 17</ref>). The general did not wear the purple embroidered robe or the laurel crown, but the ordinary <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga praetexta</foreign> and a wreath of myrtle. Moreover, he walked, or (in later times) rode on horseback, instead of riding in a chariot. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meritis_13">, masculine gender. <milestone n="246.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_igitur_13">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">gloriaris</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dixerit_13">, potential subjunctive. <milestone n="246.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gratiam_referri_13"> , <gloss>that a favor should not be returned</gloss>. <milestone n="246.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="impietatis_13">: the stories told charged Cicero with intended treason (see l.10), which would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">impietas</foreign> against his <foreign xml:lang="lat">patria</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Parilibus_14">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Parilia</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat"> Palilia</foreign> (April 21) was one of the most ancient Roman festivals, in honor of Pales, a goddess of flocks. This day was regarded as the anniversary of the founding of the city. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_dies_14">, etc., <gloss>which occur this very day</gloss>. <milestone n="247.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_descensurum_14">, i.e. was coming down to the Forum with the insignia of usurped power, as if to assume the throne. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_conlatum_14"> , <gloss>that this</gloss> [intention] <gloss>was attributed</gloss>. <pb n="489"/> <milestone n="247.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quid_14">: § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, a, N. (319, a, N.); G. 553; cf. H.-B. 502, 3, a, footnote I <milestone n="247.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_14">: if this word is retained, the expression is subj. of exclam.; § <ref n="AG 462">462</ref>, a (332, c); G. 558; H. 559, 5(486, ii, N.); H.-B. 503, a; if omitted, a rhetorical question, § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 259; H. 557 (486, ii); H.-B. 503. <milestone n="247.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exsisterem_14">, etc., <gloss>should turn out of a sudden another Catiline:</gloss> imperf., as referring back to the time when his enemies said <foreign xml:lang="lat">"descendet"</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_auspiciis_14">: the whole Roman polity was based on the assumed approval of the gods, secured in every case by auspices (<foreign xml:lang="lat">auspicia</foreign>), taken by the proper authority. Only magistrates had the right to take the auspices (see note, Cat. 4, sect., <ref n="pl142.4">p. 142, l. 4</ref>). The augur was not in any sense a magistrate, though he had the power of interpreting the auspices. Hence Cicero, though an augur, would be unable to take the first step to any usurpation of power. A technical obstacle like this would not stand long in the way of a modern usurper; but the stress here laid upon it illustrates the degree to which the peculiar formalism of the Roman religion had become worked into the Roman mind, and, further, the power that lay in this formalism to protect the institutions of the state. <milestone n="247.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="augur_14">, I, <gloss>an augur</gloss> (emphatic): i.e. an augur would know his science too well for such an attempt. This was the latest of Cicero's official honors received ten years before, and he fully appreciated the dignity of the priestly craft. <milestone n="247.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cui_traderem_14">: as the usurped authority would be illegal in its inception, so it could not be legally transferred to any successon </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quemquamne_fuisse_14">: § <ref n="AG 462">462</ref> (274); B. 334; G. 534; H. 616, iii (539, iii); H.-B. 596. <milestone n="6" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fama_15">, i.e. of Antony's success at Mutina. <milestone n="247.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illam_curiam_15">, i.e. the Pompeian: this was to the north of the Capitoline, and was the scene of Caesar's death (hence the term <foreign xml:lang="lat"> infelicem</foreign>). Fig. 52 shows the famous coin struck in commemoration of the murder of Caesar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="furiis_suis_15"> , <gloss>their own madmen</gloss>. The MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="lat"> viribus</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">juris:</foreign> Klotz's conjecture <foreign xml:lang="lat">partibus</foreign> is adopted by Halm. <milestone n="247.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_me_15">, as being now the leading man in the state. <pb n="490"/> <milestone n="247.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quasi_15">, i.e. on the pretence that. <milestone n="247.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tyrannum_15">: to the Romans <foreign xml:lang="lat">rex</foreign> and its Greek synonym <foreign xml:lang="lat">tyrannus</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="grc">τύραννος</foreign>) meant a usurper or unconstitutional monarch against whom violence would be a virtue. A dictator, though his power was practically absolute, was not a <foreign xml:lang="lat">tyrannus</foreign>, since his office was held in accordance with the ancient laws of the commonwealth. <milestone n="247.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_15"> is object and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_15"> is subject of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patefecit_15"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_inde_16"> , <gloss>ever since</gloss>. <milestone n="247.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contionem_16">: see Introd. to notes on Manil. Law. <milestone n="247.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="declaravit_16">, not by a formal vote, of course, but by spontaneous cries. <milestone n="247.32" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optatissimi_nuntii_16">, etc., i.e. of Pansa's victory at Bononia. <milestone n="248.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auxerit_16">, <gloss>added to my dignity</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="male_ageretur_17"> (a common Latin idiom),! <gloss>should fare hard</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parum_purgatus_17">, i.e. if I needed any defence against so monstrous a charge. <milestone n="248.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jejuno_angusto_17">, i.e. mean and small-souled. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_fecissem_17">: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>,3(341, d); B. 323; G. 628; H. 649, i (528, I); H.-B. 535, I, a; translate, <gloss>to do as I had always done</gloss>, [namely to] <gloss>think</gloss>, etc. <milestone n="248.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="campus_17">, etc.: observe this ancient use of a figure still familiar to us. <milestone n="248.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Crassus_17">, the great orator, who died <date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date> (Introd., p. xxxvii). <milestone n="248.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="utinam_17">, etc.: Cicero by this wish expresses his own sentiments of generous rivalry towards some of his great contemporaries, now dead, and in the same breath characterizes their feeling towards him as in like manner generous and noble. He is thinking of such great citizens as Lucullus, Hortensius, and Catulus. <milestone n="248.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_cederem_17"> , <gloss>when I myself was ready to yield to them</gloss>. <milestone n="248.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="principem_17"> , <gloss>the first man in the state</gloss>. When a Roman had held the consulship there was no higher political office in his reach, but as a member of the Senate he retained a dignified and authoritative position in the public counsels. The emulation here referred to by Cicero (<foreign xml:lang="lat">contentione principatus</foreign>, l.19) was for the first place among such men (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">consularium</foreign> in 1.14). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_tempore_17"> , i.e. now, in the dearth of strong leading men like those just referred to. <milestone n="248.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_dolore_17">, interrogative. <milestone n="248.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sententiam_moderari_17"> , <gloss>govern their views</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cursus_18">, <gloss> speed</gloss>. <pb n="491"/> <milestone n="248.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_18">, though emphatic, does not here refer to a particular person: it merely addresses the whole opposing party as if it were a single individual. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optime_sentiam_18">: cf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="male_sentire_18"> (1.15). <milestone n="248.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_fieri_18">, etc.: best rendered by changing the construction, <gloss>all good citizens gathering about me</gloss>. This refers to such occasions as that of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">gratulatio</foreign> above (<ref n="pl246.16">p. 246, l. 16</ref>). <milestone n="248.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nollem_18">, <gloss>I should regret that</gloss> (i.e. I should be sorry if that were so): Cf. § <ref n="AG 442">442</ref>, b (267, c) ; B. 280, 2, a; G. 26[, R.; H.-B. 519, 1, C. <milestone n="248.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optatius_18">, i.e. than such a course of conduct on the part of the other side. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_19">, i.e. —that <gloss>I</gloss> am the true champion of the people, and not those demagogues who are jealous of me. In this section Cicero makes an easy transition hack to the subject which he was discussing when he began to digress (at sect. <ref n="Cic. Phil. 14 13">13</ref>), — the necessity of declaring Antony a public enemy and of honoring the generals for defeating him. <milestone n="249.1" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maxime_19">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">de nobis</foreign>. <milestone n="249.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="XIII_Januarias_19"> (December 20), the day when the third and fourth Philippics were spoken, — one in the Senate and one in the Forum, -declaring Antony a public enemy. <milestone n="249.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_Januarus_19">, when, in the fifth Philippic, he urged that no negotiations should be had with Antony. The campaign against Antony may be said to have begun December 20 (see preceding note); but no active measures were taken until the new consuls entered upon their office on the first of January. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meis_sententus_20">, i.e. it was in consequence of Cicero's expressed opinion in the Senate that negotiations with Antony were broken off. It was on the question of sending an embassy (see note, sect. <ref n="Cic. Phil. 14 4">4</ref>, <ref n="pl243.7">p. 243, l. 7</ref>) to him that Cicero delivered the fifth Philippic. The embassy was sent on January I, but came to nothing, and the Senate then declared war. <milestone n="249.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illum_20">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">esse</foreign>. <milestone n="249.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_ego_20">, <gloss>just as I</gloss> [thought]. <milestone n="249.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_20">, etc. [but] <gloss>to this mere name</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Ventidium_21">, an officer of Antony's army. He afterwards gained some important successes over the Parthians, <date when="-0038">B.C. 38</date>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="volusenum_21">: the manuscripts here are hopelessly corrupt. <milestone n="249.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="discessionem_21"> , <gloss>"division"</gloss> (as in the English House of Commons); see general Introd., p. lvii. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluissent_21">: the presiding consuls could put a question to vote in the Senate—or not—at their discretion, <pb n="492"/> since they alone were regarded as having the initiative in deliberations (see general Introd., p. lvii). <milestone n="249.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="licuit_21">, i.e. by the consuls (see last note). <milestone n="249.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verbis_notari_21">: spurious, and to be disregarded in translation. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sustulerunt_22">, i.e. refused to put that question. <milestone n="249.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imprudens_22"> , <gloss>without knowing it</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bellum_Octavianum_23">: see Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.24">24</ref> (<ref n="pl137.26">p. 137, l. 26</ref>) and note. <milestone n="250.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Servili_23">: see note on <ref n="pl244.9">p. 244, l. 9</ref>. <milestone n="250.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlega_23">, i.e. Julius Caesar. <milestone n="250.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_Alexandria_23">: for a victory over the Egyptians; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_23"> Pharnace, son of Mithridates, King of Pontus (both victories, <date when="-0047">B.C. 47</date>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ob_conservationem_24">: see Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.15">15</ref>. <milestone n="250.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gabinium_24">: he had claimed a <foreign xml:lang="lat">supplicatio</foreign>, which the Senate steadily refused, for some successes against Arab marauders in Syria. <milestone n="250.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="re_24">, <gloss>in effect</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verbo_24">, <gloss>in so many words</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoris_amplissimi_25">, i.e. the consulship. <milestone n="250.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterum_25">, i.e. consul; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterum_25_2">, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator</foreign>. <milestone n="250.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_membris_25">, etc.: Antony would not only cut their throats but treat their bodies with indignity, — as was, in fact, afterwards done in the case of Cicero. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="With_Sect_26">, 25 is a transition from the argumentative part of the oration to the laudatory portion. <milestone n="250.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
lemma: With this section the formal eulogy begins. Sect

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legione_Martia_26">: this was one of two legions that had gone over from Antony to the Senate the November previous. The other was the <foreign xml:lang="lat">quarta</foreign>, mentioned below (<ref n="pl251.11">p. 251, l. 11</ref>). <milestone n="251.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterum_26">, referring to the second alternative (<foreign xml:lang="lat">victoria se</foreign>, etc.), according to the favorite Latin chiastic order. <milestone n="10" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="beneficia_27">, i.e. grants of money and assignments of land to Julius Caesar's veterans. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servassent_27"> , <gloss>hod saved</gloss>, i.e. had <pb n="493"/> not, like some others, wasted their property and become reprobates (cf. Cat. 2, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.20">20</ref>). <milestone n="251.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viginti_cohortibus_27">, i.e. two legions. <milestone n="251.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribus_27">: in point of fact, Antony had only two legions engaged; but full particulars had not yet reached Rome, and Cicero appears to have thought that a third legion, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Alauda</foreign>, which he had with him, took part in the fight. <milestone n="251.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_27">, etc., dep. on <foreign xml:lang="lat">imminentis</foreign> (1.21). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aetas_28">: Octavianus was now twenty years old, an age at which no person could regularly hold the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>. <milestone n="252.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejus_nominis_28"> , <gloss>that title (<foreign xml:lang="lat">imperator</foreign> </gloss>). This, though connected with the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign>, was not conferred with that power, but followed some important success in the field, being given by acclamation of the soldiers. <milestone n="252.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="castra_28">, i.e. the camp of Hirtius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decerno_29">, <gloss>I propose</gloss>: note that this word often does not mean <gloss>decree</gloss>, but is used of a single Senator, —<gloss>vote</gloss> or <gloss>propose</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quinquaginta_29">, an unprecedented number. A ten days' <foreign xml:lang="lat">supplicatio</foreign> had been decreed for Pompey's victories in Africa, and fifteen for Caesar's defeat of the Belgians. <milestone n="252.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_29">, <gloss>it is due</gloss> to. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fidei_declarare_29">: § <ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, c (214, d); B. 198, 3; G. 366, R.2; H. 447 (403); cf. H.-B. 340. <milestone n="252.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bello_confecto_29">: § <ref n="AG 420">420</ref> (255, d); B. 237, 2; G. 410; H. 489, I (43 I, I); H.-B. 421, 3; notice (he reference to future time. <milestone n="252.21" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conjungi_29">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum honore imperatorum</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnibus_30">, i.e. to all, both living and dead. To the living the full reward is due only on the completion of the war; to the dead, however, it can be paid at once by being given to their heirs (see the end of the decree, sect. <ref n="Cic. Phil. 14 36">36</ref>). <milestone n="252.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victoribus_30">, i.e. at the end of the campaign. <milestone n="252.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_sunt_30">, i.e. relying on which (the pledge of the Senate), they followed the cause. <milestone n="252.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consili_sui_30"> , <gloss>their course</gloss> (i.e. their espousal of the cause of good order). <milestone n="252.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_30">, i.e. the living, whose silent presence is a reminder. <milestone n="252.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatus_sapientis_30">: the Senate, as composed of the wisest citizens, would best appreciate the importance of encouraging patriotism. <pb n="494"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occurrunt_31"> , <gloss>suggest themselves</gloss>. <milestone n="253.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placet_mihi_31"> , <gloss>my proposition is</gloss> (an almost technical use of the phrase: see Vocab.). <milestone n="253.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="se_abrupit_31">: Cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Phil. 14 26">26</ref>. <milestone n="253.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Albam_31">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">Fucensem</foreign>: a town among the mountains, in the territory of the Marsi, which the Martian Legion took and held after revolting from Antony. <milestone n="253.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desiderat_31"> , <gloss>has lost</gloss> <milestone n="253.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_victoria_31"> , <gloss>at the moment of victory</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vos_32">: here he addresses the Martian Legion. <milestone n="253.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_deus_32">: Mars was not only the special patron god of Rome, but, being the father of Romulus, was regarded as the ancestor of the Roman race (hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">urbem genuisse</foreign>). <milestone n="13" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="publice_34">, i.e. by way of public eulogy. <milestone n="254.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bustis_34">, <gloss> burial-mounds</gloss>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">bustum</foreign> was properly the heap of ashes left after the body had been consumed with the <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogris</foreign> ; but the term was also applied to the mound erected on the spot where the body was burned. For an elaborate <foreign xml:lang="lat">bustum</foreign>, see the round tomb of Caecilia Metella in the view of the Appian Way (text, p.169). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sententia_36">, i.e. a formal proposition for a decree (analogous to "a motion reduced to writing " in a modern deliberative assembly). In the Roman Senate questions were proposed only by a magistrate; and this was done not in the form of a set motion, as with us, but the whole question was presented (<foreign xml:lang="lat">de re referre</foreign>) for discussion. The result of the deliberation might be several formal propositions for a decree, all, any, or none of which might be formally put to vote by the presiding officer. If one of these was carried, it would stand as the <foreign xml:lang="lat">senatus consultum</foreign>. (Cf. Introd., p. lvii, above). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="complectar_36">, i.e. <gloss>my views on the whole question</gloss>. <milestone n="255.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_36">, <gloss> whereas</gloss>. <milestone n="255.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occidione_occident_36">: notice the set phrase, not used in ordinary language. Translate, <gloss>cut to pieces with great slaughter</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatum_judicare_37">, indir. disc. depending on <foreign xml:lang="lat">censeo</foreign> (l. 7); in the decree it would be <foreign xml:lang="lat">senatus . . . judicat</foreign> <milestone n="255.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uti_constituat_37">: in the decree this would depend on some word of commanding (like <foreign xml:lang="lat">decernit</foreign>) in the heading; hence it stands unchanged in Cicero's indirect statement. <milestone n="255.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alter_ambove_37">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> of the two consuls was absolutely equal, and the power of neither was impaired by the special assignment of any duty to the other. Any such special assignment of functions was only made by mutual consent, and either had a legal right to interfere in the other's province. Of course, however, any such interference was regarded as unwarranted, and in practice the two colleagues either took turns in the administration or agreed upon a division of functions between them. <milestone n="255.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pulvinaria_37">: see note on Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.23">23</ref> (<ref n="pl137.14">p. 137, l. 14</ref>). A <foreign xml:lang="lat"> supplicatio</foreign> was one of the few religious rites of the Romans in which the whole people took part. The proper temples were opened and the gods symbolically served with a feast The citizens repaired to these temples and paid their individual devotions to the gods in peculiar forms of humiliation not ordinarily observed in the public sacrifices. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatum_soluturum_38">: here the statement returns to the form of the indir. disc., — in the decree, <foreign xml:lang="lat">senatus . . solvet</foreign>. <milestone n="255.33" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_caederent_38">, concessive. <milestone n="256.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locandum_curent_38">: see note on Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.20">20</ref> (<ref n="pl136.8">p. 136, l. 8</ref>). <milestone n="256.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_vicissent_38">, if <gloss>they had survived their victory</gloss>. </gloss>
